kodava: naan poanê.
naan poanê
english: I went — naan = I, poanê = went (1st person past of poap'k)
note: poanê is 1st person past. poochi is 3rd person — never use poochi for I went
explanation: poanê = I went. How do I say I went in Kodava? Past tense first person of poap'k (to go). poanê is the 1st person past form. naan poanê = I went. Never use poochi for I went.

kodava: naan sante'k poanê
english: I went to the market — naan = I, sante = market, 'k = to/toward, poanê = went
note: sante = weekly open market. Suffix 'k attaches with apostrophe — never -yak
explanation: I went to the market in Kodava — naan = I, sante = market (weekly open market), 'k = to/toward (dative suffix), poanê = went. Market in Kodava is sante.

query: how does the 'k suffix work direction
kodava: sante'k, angadi'k, mane'k, devasthana'k
english: to the market, to the store, to the house, to the temple — 'k suffix marks direction
note: 'k attaches directly with apostrophe. Never insert -ya- between noun and 'k

query: I will go tomorrow future tense going
kodava: naan naaLe Kavadi'k poapo.
english: I will go to Kavadi tomorrow — naan = I, naaLe = tomorrow, Kavadi'k = to Kavadi, poapo = will go (1st person future/going form of poap'k)
note: poapo is the 1st person future/non-past form. Same pattern: naan bappa waara poapo = I am going next week.
explanation: I will go tomorrow in Kodava — future tense. naan = I, naaLe = tomorrow, poapo = will go (1st person future of poap'k).

kodava: naaLe, mathyaandh, indh
english: tomorrow = naaLe, day after tomorrow = mathyaandh, today = indh
note: time adverbs placed before the destination in Kodava SOV word order: Subject + Time + Destination + Verb

query: I will not go won't go definite negative future
kodava: naan naaLe office'k poap'le.
english: I will not go to the office tomorrow — poap'le = definite negative future of poap'k
note: poap'le = will not go (definite). Different from poap'killê which is dubitative (may not go)
explanation: I will not go in Kodava — definite negative future. poap'le = will not go (definite negative future of poap'k).

query: I may not go might not go dubitative negative
kodava: naan naaLe office'k poap'killê.
english: I may not go to the office tomorrow — poap'killê = dubitative negative future of poap'k
note: poap'killê = may not go (uncertain/dubitative). Different from poap'le which is definite.
explanation: I may not go in Kodava — dubitative negative future. poap'killê = may not go (uncertain negative future of poap'k).

query: days of the week in Kodava aache
kodava: thingLLaache (Mon), chavvaache (Tue), padhnaache (Wed), baaLLaache (Thu), boLLiaache (Fri), cheniaache (Sat), naaraache (Sun)
devanagari: थिंगळ्ळाचे, चव्वाचे, पाध्नाचे, बाळ्ळाचे, बोळ्ळियाचे, चेनियाचे, नाराचे
kannada: ತಿಂಗಳ್ಳಾಚೆ, ಚವ್ವಾಚೆ, ಪಾದ್ನಾಚೆ, ಬಾಳ್ಳಾಚೆ, ಬೊಳ್ಳಿಯಾಚೆ, ಚೆನಿಯಾಚೆ, ನಾರಾಚೆ
english: Days of the week: Monday=thingLLaache, Tuesday=chavvaache, Wednesday=padhnaache, Thursday=baaLLaache, Friday=boLLiaache, Saturday=cheniaache, Sunday=naaraache
explanation: Kodava days of the week. All day names end in -aache. waarathra dina = days of the week. Monday=thingLLaache, Tuesday=chavvaache, Wednesday=padhnaache, Thursday=baaLLaache, Friday=boLLiaache, Saturday=cheniaache, Sunday=naaraache. Devanagari Hindi script forms available.
note: Kodava day names all end in -aache. NOT the Sanskrit/Kannada -vaara forms. waarathra dina = days of the week

kodava: polaaka namaskara
english: good morning (greeting)

kodava: bayt namaskara
english: good evening / good night (greeting)
explanation: Evening or night greeting: bayt (evening/night) + namaskara (greetings).

kodava: sante'k
english: to the market

kodava: mane'k
english: to the house

kodava: maadiye
english: did / made (past tense)
explanation: Derivation example: maadiye → मादिये (past tense verb)

kodava: maaduwii
english: does / makes (nonpast)

kodava: kuuL'ra
english: of the river (genitive)

kodava: naa'k kela baichaind'und
english: I am hungry
explanation: naa'k = to me (dative), kela = stomach, baichaind'und = present progressive of baip'w

kodava: kela baip'w
english: I am hungry / stomach is growling (hungry)
explanation: kela = stomach, baip'w = hungry. Variants: kela baipa, kela baichaind'und

kodava: naa'k kela baip'w
english: I am hungry
explanation: naa'k = dative of naan, kela = stomach, baip'w = hungry

kodava: aad, palaaka êêL gante naa êêwi. ille, naada breakfast'k inyii minynya naa baayi kaththuwii. pinynya naa kelsa'k poapii. aad, palaaka êtt gante'nja majjana anji gante'k eththane kelsa maaduwii. naa kelsa maaduwii, pinynya mane'k bappi. naa naada dinner thimbii pinynya pathth gante'l waruwii.
english: daily routine paragraph: I wake up at 7 in the morning. Before breakfast I brush my teeth. Then I go to work. I work from 8am to 5pm. I work, then come home. I eat dinner then at 10 I sleep.
explanation: daily routine paragraph in Kodava. Connectives: aad (alright/well), pinynya (then/next/after that), minynya (before), ille (no — used here in 'before breakfast'). Vocabulary: palaaka=morning, êêwi=wake up, baayi kaththuwii=brush teeth, kelsa=work, poapii=go, mane=home, dinner thimbii=eat dinner, waruwii=sleep. Source: SAMPLE DIALOGUE from elementary_kodava_FINAL.md lesson 8.

kodava: en visheasha? chips uNdaa? uNd. eadh flavor? eadh'ella flavors uNd? masala, chili, onion, 'ella uNd. serii. andhaka oar masala. oar packet echchak?
english: market snack exchange paragraph: Hi, are there chips? There are. Which flavor? Which all flavors are there? Masala, chili, onion, all are there. OK. One masala. How much is one packet?
explanation: market shopping paragraph in Kodava. Going to the market, buying snacks, asking about items at a vendor. Connectives: serii, andhaka.

kodava: ennane uLLira ninga? chaayi'l uLL. nii Madikeri'k poapiya? akku. illi'nja Madikeri'k eththane echcha dhuura? sumaar nooita irovadh kilometer. echchak gante'k Madikeri'k ettho? sumaar muundh gante. serii. nin Madikeri'nja bandhand ulliya? ille — naa Bengaluru'nja bandandh ullo.
english: travel conversation paragraph: How are you? I'm well. Are you going to Madikeri? Yes. How far is it from here to Madikeri? About 120 kilometers. How many hours to reach Madikeri? About 3 hours. Alright. Were you coming from Madikeri? No — I was coming from Bengaluru.
explanation: travel and directions paragraph in Kodava. Connectives: akku (yes/agreed), serii (alright), ille (no/but not). Vocabulary: ennane uLLira=how are you, chaayi'l=well, poapiya=going, dhuura=far, eththane=how many/how much, sumaar=about/approximately, ettho=reach/arrive. Source: SAMPLE DIALOGUE from elementary_kodava_FINAL.md lesson 5.

kodava: nii ead kelsa maaduwiiyaa? minynya, naa advertising'l kelsa maadiye. niin? naa I.T.'l injiye. nii college'l elli woadhiiyaa? naa Michigan'l oar university'l woadhiye. Communication and Advertising maadiye. Mysuru'l oar university'l woodhiye. alli naa computer science woadhiye.
english: self-introduction paragraph: What work do you do? Before I was in advertising. You? I was in IT. Where did you study in college? I studied at a university in Michigan. I studied Communication and Advertising. I studied at a university in Mysuru. There I studied computer science.
explanation: self-introduction paragraph in Kodava. Introducing yourself. Talking about work and education background. Past tense conversation. Connective: minynya (before/previously).

kodava: serii, êdhdhiith aana pinynya dhivsa'thra wondhane sangathii oar bisi filter kaapi. dhandane sangathii: porme'l nadndhan poakana. muundhane sangathii: lunch'k nanga kadambutt beembaLe karii thingana.
english: activities planning paragraph in Kodava: After getting up, the first thing is a hot filter coffee. Second thing: we shall go for a walk outside. Third thing: for lunch we shall have kaDambuTT and bamboo curry.
explanation: activities planning paragraph in Kodava. Things to do in Kodagu. Daily activities schedule. Morning routine in Kodagu. Connectives: serii, aana pinynya, wondhane dhandane muundhane (first second third).

kodava: pinynya naa mane'l ennane ikk'uNdaa? serii. minynya, vays aana aing'na paadhdhana maaduwii. pinynya naa mane'l ennane ikk'uNdaa? serii. pinynya naa thimb'k aap'ka naa entha'ella sangathii maaduwuNdaa? andhaka oar thank you'k inyii jaasthi êNNuwii.
english: house visit etiquette paragraph: So how should I be inside the house? Alright. First you must touch the feet of elders. So how should I act inside the house? Alright. So while I'm eating, what all should I do? More than a thank you, one should say [more].
explanation: Kodava house visit etiquette paragraph. Connectives: pinynya (so/then/after that), serii (alright), minynya (first/before), andhaka (more than/and so). Vocabulary: mane'l=inside the house, ikk'uNdaa=should be/how to be, vays=age/elders, paadhdhana maaduwii=touch the feet, thimb'k=eating. Source: SAMPLE DIALOGUE from elementary_kodava_FINAL.md lesson 12.

kodava: Ellarku nada thakkara. Naan Manavttira Pramod Poovaiah. Naan Kodava Koota of North America'thra Board of Directors'lu obba. Ningellariku Maathayi Kaavêramme nallad maadad'ndu kêtt'ndu ee video'na shuru maaduvi. Ikkaakale nangara namme naadira bagge nanga sumaar videos maadittu. Idhu ningak'ke khushi aayind'ndu kêttu bandittu. Naada koode indu Monnanda Nithin injathe. Ellarku Changrandi nammera nallaame. Kaavêri Shankramana'thra visheshathe enth? Ee nammena nanga ennange kaippu? Ee nammera bagge noanoad aachingi nangak'ke Kaavêrira kathena chenna arinjoand chaapa. Kaavêri Brahmanda moava Lopamudra'ndu kaako enjath. Aachingi Kavêrara noava chaaki boLathina uh kannina, adhan'aytthu Kavêrara moava Kaavêri en'ndu nanga kaakwa. Aa kannik'ke ekkolu naanu loka kalyaanake naada jeevana en'ndu mansil bhaari aaLavaaytthu injath injath. BaLyoaLaaytthu chaaykarthi aaytthu boLandaninjoavaLa Agasthya muni kandittu kodhiyaaytthu nanna mangalaappiya en'ndu kêttava. Akka Kaavêri, nana mangalaappak'ke naada ooru... ooru sharaththu injathe en'ndu eNNuwa. EntheN'chingi, mangalaanaannalli nana oor kshana aachingyu neenu dhoora bechôkaaga. Ekkolu neera jothele nana bechôndu en'ndu eNNittu Agasthya muni otthandu mangalaappa. Kaavêrira mansil maathra loka kalyaanave maadôndu en'ndu ippa. Oor dhivasa Agasthya muni thapasuk'ke poapaka Kaavêrina neeraaythu parivarthane maadittu thaanra kamandulul' ittandu Brahmmagiri kundil thapasuk'ke poayittu Brahmmagiri kundira kôdil adhana aa kamandaluna bechittu poLel kuLippak'ke poapa. Idhe samayathina noattiyandu Ganapathi kaake roopathil bandronja ee kamandaluna dhoodittu marichiruva. Idhana... idhana kandronja Kodagul'uLLa jana, Kodoava baaki moola nivaasi ella poayittu ee Balambêril poLena dhêvina thadapak'ke poapa. Adhu oru kathe injathe. Akka poLe... aa neerarira okko rabasak'ke podiya o'thra neri minjyathlinjath bayyak'ke poaye. Annane nanga Kodoava, Kodoavathiya bayyak'ke neri bechittu podiyo'dapak'ke shuruvaachi'ndu. Achchan katheyil entha uLLaN'chingi, allinja dhêvina nangala buttittu neenu poayirthaa en'ndu ainga praarthane maadiyava. Akka Kaavêri eNNuwa, naan ningala buttittu elliku poayandille, naan ningara jeevanak'ke anukoola aapanekke poLe aaythu okkiyanduLL. Kaala komma, naan bandittu, theertha roopini aaythu bandronja, ningak'kella aasheervaada maaduvi en'ndu eNNuwa. Ee Kaavêri dhêvi theerthava roopini aaythu banth'badhe naanga sankramana athava changrandi namme en'ndu kaippa.
english: Greetings to all. I am Pramod Poovaiah from Manavattira. I serve on the Board of Directors of the Kodava Koota of North America. I begin this video by praying to Mother Kaavêri to bless everyone with good fortune. Until now, we have made several videos about our homeland. Hearing that these have brought you joy, we have come back with more. Joining me today is Nithin from Monnanda. Wishing everyone a joyous Changrandi. What is the significance of the Kaavêri Shankramana festival? How do we, as a people, relate to this festival? In thinking about this festival, it becomes clear that we must know the story of Kaavêri well. Kaavêri was born of Brahma's kamandalu. She is also known as Lopamudra. It is said that when the sage longed for Kaavêri, she emerged as a stream from his eye — and from that tearful stream, she who flowed forth came to be called Kaavêri by us. From that very moment, she has lived on in the hearts of people, deeply and endlessly, as a life devoted to the welfare of the world. Having grown up and come of age, the radiant Kaaveri was spotted by the sage Agasthya, who, smitten by her beauty, asked what auspicious blessing she sought. Kaaveri replied that for her auspicious wish, she desired a land and a people — and wondered whether there was any place worthy of that. She then said: in the moment of my auspicious union, do not keep me away even for an instant. Thinking that he would always keep her close like water beside him, the sage Agasthya agreed to the marriage. It is said that Kaaveri's heart held only one desire — the welfare of all the world. One day, before leaving for his tapas, the sage Agasthya transformed Kaaveri into water, placed her in his kamandalu, set it on the peak of Brahmmagiri, and went off to perform his tapas; having left the kamandalu on the edge of Brahmmagiri's peak, he went to bathe in the open stream. At that very moment, Ganapathi, having noticed this, came in the form of a crow and tipped over that kamandalu, causing it to spill. Seeing this, all the people of Kodagu — the Kodavas and all the original inhabitants — went to the banks of that river flowing at Balambêr to behold the divine presence. That is, in itself, a story. The river — with each surge of those waters — flowed swiftly, wave after wave, spilling over the banks. And so it is that we Kodavas began the tradition of going to the riverbank and offering worship at the water's edge. In the original story, those who were there prayed to the divine, saying: you who gave us life, where have you gone, please do not leave us. Kaaveri replied: I have not left you and gone anywhere — I have settled here as a river to sustain your lives. In time, I will return, and when I come back in the form of sacred waters, I will bless you all. This occasion when Kaaveri Devi returns as sacred waters — we call it Sankramana, or Changrandi, as it is known to us.
explanation: Kaveri Sankramana introduction paragraph. Kodava festival greeting. Pramod Poovaiah introduces the Kaveri Changrandi festival. Native speaker audio.

kodava: Ellarku nada thakkara.
english: Greetings to everyone.

kodava: Naan Manavttira Pramod Poovaiah.
english: I am Pramod Poovaiah from Manavattira.

kodava: Naan Kodava Koota of North America'thra Board of Directors'lu obba.
english: I am a member of the Board of Directors of the Kodava Koota of North America.

kodava: Ningellariku Maathayi Kaavêramme nallad maadad'ndu kêtt'ndu ee video'na shuru maaduvi.
english: I begin this video by praying to Mother Kaavêri to bestow her blessings upon all of you.

kodava: Ikkaakale nangara namme naadira bagge nanga sumaar videos maadittu.
english: Until now, we have made quite a few videos about our homeland.

kodava: Idhu ningak'ke khushi aayind'ndu kêttu bandittu.
english: Having heard that these have brought you happiness, we have returned with more.

kodava: Naada koode indu Monnanda Nithin injathe.
english: Joining me today is Nithin from Monnanda.

kodava: Ellarku Changrandi nammera nallaame.
english: Wishing everyone a joyous and auspicious Changrandi.

kodava: Kaavêri Shankramana'thra visheshathe enth?
english: What is the special significance of the Kaavêri Shankramana festival?

kodava: Ee nammena nanga ennange kaippu?
english: What is our connection to this festival as a people?

kodava: Ee nammera bagge noanoad aachingi nangak'ke Kaavêrira kathena chenna arinjoand chaapa.
english: When reflecting on this festival, it becomes clear that we must know the story of Kaavêri well.

kodava: Kaavêri Brahmanda moava
english: Kaavêri was born from Brahma's kamandalu —

kodava: Lopamudra'ndu kaako enjath.
english: she is also known as Lopamudra.

kodava: Aachingi Kavêrara noava chaaki boLathina uh kannina, adhan'aytthu Kavêrara moava Kaavêri en'ndu nanga kaakwa.
english: It is said that when the sage yearned deeply for Kaavêri, she flowed forth as a stream from his eye — and from that tearful stream she emerged, and that is why we call her Kaavêri.

kodava: Aa kannik'ke ekkolu naanu loka kalyaanake naada jeevana en'ndu mansil bhaari aaLavaaytthu injath injath.
english: From that very moment, she has dwelled profoundly in the hearts of the people, as a life lived for the welfare of the world.

kodava: BaLyoaLaaytthu chaaykarthi aaytthu boLandaninjoavaLa Agasthya muni kandittu kodhiyaaytthu nanna mangalaappiya en'ndu kêttava.
english: Having grown up and blossomed into a radiant young woman, Kaaveri was seen by the sage Agasthya, who was captivated by her and asked her what auspicious blessing she desired.

kodava: Akka Kaavêri, nana mangalaappak'ke naada ooru... ooru sharaththu injathe en'ndu eNNuwa.
english: Kaaveri thought to herself: for my auspicious wish, I need a land, a people — and wondered whether there was truly such a place worthy of it.

kodava: EntheN'chingi, mangalaanaannalli nana oor kshana aachingyu neenu dhoora bechôkaaga.
english: She said: whatever happens, at the moment of my auspicious union, do not keep me away from you even for an instant.

kodava: Ekkolu neera jothele nana bechôndu en'ndu eNNittu Agasthya muni otthandu mangalaappa.
english: Thinking that he would always keep her close, just as water stays beside one, the sage Agasthya agreed and the auspicious union took place.

kodava: Kaavêrira mansil maathra loka kalyaanave maadôndu en'ndu ippa.
english: It is said that in Kaaveri's heart, there was only one wish — to bring welfare and well-being to all the world.

kodava: Oor dhivasa Agasthya muni thapasuk'ke poapaka Kaavêrina neeraaythu parivarthane maadittu thaanra kamandulul' ittandu Brahmmagiri kundil thapasuk'ke poayittu Brahmmagiri kundira kôdil adhana aa kamandaluna bechittu poLel kuLippak'ke poapa.
english: One day, before leaving for his tapas, the sage Agasthya transformed Kaaveri into water, placed her within his kamandalu, went to Brahmmagiri to perform his tapas, left the kamandalu on the edge of the Brahmmagiri peak, and went to bathe in the open stream.

kodava: Idhe samayathina noattiyandu Ganapathi kaake roopathil bandronja ee kamandaluna dhoodittu marichiruva.
english: At that very moment, having noticed this, Ganapathi came in the form of a crow and tipped over the kamandalu, causing it to spill.

kodava: Idhana... idhana kandronja Kodagul'uLLa jana, Kodoava baaki moola nivaasi ella poayittu ee Balambêril poLena dhêvina thadapak'ke poapa.
english: Seeing this, all the people of Kodagu — the Kodavas and all the original inhabitants — went to the banks of that river at Balambêr to witness and seek the divine presence.

kodava: Adhu oru kathe injathe.
english: That itself is a story of its own.

kodava: Akka poLe... aa neerarira okko rabasak'ke podiya o'thra neri minjyathlinjath bayyak'ke poaye.
english: And the river — with each surge of those waters — flowed swiftly, wave after wave spilling over and rushing to the banks.

kodava: Annane nanga Kodoava, Kodoavathiya bayyak'ke neri bechittu podiyo'dapak'ke shuruvaachi'ndu.
english: And so it is that we Kodavas began the tradition of going to the riverbank and offering worship at the water's edge.

kodava: Achchan katheyil entha uLLaN'chingi, allinja dhêvina nangala buttittu neenu poayirthaa en'ndu ainga praarthane maadiyava.
english: According to the original story, those who were there prayed to the divine, saying: you who gave birth to us and sustained us, where have you gone, please do not leave us.

kodava: Akka Kaavêri eNNuwa, naan ningala buttittu elliku poayandille, naan ningara jeevanak'ke anukoola aapanekke poLe aaythu okkiyanduLL.
english: Kaaveri replied: I have not left you and gone anywhere — I have settled here as a river, so as to be favorable and sustaining to your lives.

kodava: Kaala komma, naan bandittu, theertha roopini aaythu bandronja, ningak'kella aasheervaada maaduvi en'ndu eNNuwa.
english: She said: in due time, I will return — and when I come back in the form of sacred waters, I will bless you all.

kodava: Ee Kaavêri dhêvi theerthava roopini aaythu banth'badhe naanga sankramana athava changrandi namme en'ndu kaippa.
english: The occasion when the goddess Kaaveri returns and arrives in the form of sacred waters — this we call Sankramana, or as it is known to us, Changrandi.

kodava: Kaavêri Changraandi eedh dhinath'th bappa? idhu Kodoava naala th'orap'le edthanthengi, êLane thinga, Tholeyaar thingathil bappantha namme. SaadhaaraNa aaythu idhu October padhinêLane thaareek athava October 17th'k bappa. Soorya Thula raashik'ke pravesha maaduvantha punya ghaLigêl' Kaavêri thaayi theertha roopini aaythu Thalakaavêril'uLLa Brahma kundikêl' banthu okkuva. Idhe ghaLigêl' Kaavêri ShankramaNathina nanga nadathuva.
english: On what day does Kaavêri Changraandi fall? According to the Kodava calendar, it comes in the seventh month, in the latter half of the month of Tholeyaar. Ordinarily, this falls on the seventeenth of October, or October 17th. At the auspicious moment when the Sun enters the Thula raashi, Kaavêri mother manifests as sacred water (theertha roopini) and arrives in the Brahma kundiké at Talakaveri. It is at this very moment that we celebrate the Kaavêri ShankramaNa festival.
explanation: Kaveri Sankramana festival paragraph. When does Kaveri Changrandi happen? Kodava calendar, Tholeyaar month, October 17, Thula raashi. Native speaker audio explanation of the festival date.

kodava: Kaavêri Changraandi eedh dhinath'th bappa?
english: On which day does Kaavêri Changraandi come?

kodava: idhu Kodoava naala th'orap'le edthanthengi, êLane thinga, Tholeyaar thingathil bappantha namme.
english: Reckoning by the Kodava calendar, it comes in the second half of the seventh month, the month of Tholeyaar.

kodava: SaadhaaraNa aaythu idhu October padhinêLane thaareek athava October 17th'k bappa.
english: Generally, this falls on the seventeenth of October, that is, October 17th.

kodava: Soorya Thula raashik'ke pravesha maaduvantha punya ghaLigêl' Kaavêri thaayi theertha roopini aaythu Thalakaavêril'uLLa Brahma kundikêl' banthu okkuva.
english: At the auspicious moment when the Sun enters the Thula raashi, Mother Kaavêri manifests as sacred water (theertha roopini) and wells up in the Brahma kundiké at Talakaveri.

kodava: Idhe ghaLigêl' Kaavêri ShankramaNathina nanga nadathuva.
english: It is at this very auspicious moment that we observe the Kaavêri ShankramaNa festival.

kodava: Nithin, Kodoava janaanga Changraandina ennane kaippa? Indathra ee shubha dhinathil' Thalakaavêrik'ke poayittu Brahma kundikêl' theerthathil' kuLichittu manek'ke theertha edtthan bappadhu nalladhu en'ndu nangadellaara nambike. Andhane kai vachak'ke jana Thalakaavêrik'ke poayittu ee kaarya maaduva. Aachingi andh' allik'ke poayik'ke etthô kaiyêthanthainga Paalooril'uLLantha Harishchandra dhêvasthaanakku pinya Balambêril'uLLa dhêvasthaanakku poayittu kuLichith bappantha paddhathiyu injathe. Adh allathe, Changraandi and poapakaiyyathavu, allinja oor thinga kathane, ee minjyathra thinga enchingi Birchiyaar thingathra ondanê dhivasa, adhana Krith Changraandi'ndu koodi eNNuwa. Akka ê dhivasa aachingyu aingak'k anukoola uLLanekke poayittu idhu Kaavêril' kuLichittu theertha manek'ke edthan baralu en'ndu nanga nambith.
english: Nithin asks how the Kodava people celebrate Changraandi. The belief among all of us is that on this auspicious day, it is good to go to Talakaveri, bathe in the sacred waters of the Brahma kundi, and bring back the holy water (theertha) for the home. Following this tradition, people go to Talakaveri to perform this sacred act. However, in earlier times, before travel to Talakaveri was as accessible as it is today, people would go to the Harishchandra temple in Paalooru or the temple in Balamberu, bathe there, and return — this was the customary practice. Apart from this, the month preceding Changraandi, which is the current month, and the month of Birchiyaar that follows — together these count as one day, and that day is called Krith Changraandi. It is our belief that on that day too, those who are able should go and bathe in the Kaaveri and bring back the holy water (theertha) to the home.
explanation: Kaveri Sankramana celebration paragraph. How Kodava people celebrate Changrandi. Pilgrimage to Talakaveri, Brahma kundi, theertha water, holy bath. Native speaker audio.

kodava: Nithin, Kodoava janaanga Changraandina ennane kaippa?
english: Nithin, how do the Kodava people celebrate Changraandi?

kodava: Indathra ee shubha dhinathil' Thalakaavêrik'ke poayittu Brahma kundikêl' theerthathil' kuLichittu manek'ke theertha edtthan bappadhu nalladhu en'ndu nangadellaara nambike.
english: It is the belief of all of us that on this auspicious day, one should go to Talakaveri, bathe in the sacred theertha of the Brahma kundi, and bring back the holy water to the home.

kodava: Andhane kai vachak'ke jana Thalakaavêrik'ke poayittu ee kaarya maaduva.
english: Following this tradition, people go to Talakaveri to carry out this sacred observance.

kodava: Aachingi andh' allik'ke poayik'ke etthô kaiyêthanthainga Paalooril'uLLantha Harishchandra dhêvasthaanakku pinya Balambêril'uLLa dhêvasthaanakku poayittu kuLichith bappantha paddhathiyu injathe.
english: In earlier times, when going there was not so easily accessible, the practice was to go to the Harishchandra temple in Paalooru or the temple in Balamberu, bathe there, and return.

kodava: Adh allathe, Changraandi and poapakaiyyathavu, allinja oor thinga kathane, ee minjyathra thinga enchingi Birchiyaar thingathra ondanê dhivasa, adhana Krith Changraandi'ndu koodi eNNuwa.
english: Besides that, the month that precedes Changraandi — that is, the previous month — and this current month along with the month of Birchiyaar together count as one single day, and that is also reckoned as Krith Changraandi.

kodava: Akka ê dhivasa aachingyu aingak'k anukoola uLLanekke poayittu idhu Kaavêril' kuLichittu theertha manek'ke edthan baralu en'ndu nanga nambith.
english: We believe that on that day too, those who are able should go, bathe in the Kaaveri, and bring back the theertha to the home.

kodava: Nithin, pinya botthu kuttho eN'chingi enth? Adara visheshathe enth'dha chenna'NNithari? Pramod, botth eNNo padathra artha, poadi Ee botth'ra bagge naanu dhandu kathe eNNod'k kêttiye. Ondu enthaN'chingi ee Tholeyaar thingathil' natta bêlil boLe bandittu, nellu porame bappantha samaya. Akka ee pakshi ella bandittu adhana thindittu kardu maaduva, adhana dhoora bekkôndu en'ndu ee botth'na kutthuvanthadhu naanu eNNodhu kêtti. Ee botth enthaN'chingi, marathra koalil' koalu, adhu sumaar jaagathil' ponge mara eNNo oru marathra koalina anga baLisiyava. Aanda kôdik'ke kaibili baLLi... adhu kaibili baLLina ennange koottiyôNa adhara thoppu sumaar samayakathane'k'ke oNangathe pachche aaythe ippalo. Adhana suththittu botthaaythu adhana beppa. Boare... achchengi sumaar boare ooril' boare boare marathina anga poNena ella koottiyava on sametha naanu eNNodhu kêttiye. Adhu bêlil allathe, baaki mukhyapatta sthaana, enchingi manera pattiil', pinya kanthaarera pakka, patthaayathra pakka, chaaNakundralli, ella beppa. Adhu enangan'chingi, ennengi dhushta shakthira kaNNu paduvantha injathengi, adhana dhoora bekkôndu en'ndu botthu kutthuva en'ndu kêttiye. Adhallathe inyor kathe entha uLLa enchingi ee Paandavanga vanavaasathra samayathil' Kodagu'k banthu aingak'ke baari Kodaguna baari khushi aayrth'lo. Akka nanga idhana nangara raajyak'ke koottiyôndu eNNeN'ndu avu Kaavêri thaayina poayith ee jaagak'kêkaa'ndu aingara madhyathile thakka bar'nand'ippaka, alli sutthu mutthu inja Kodoava adhana kêttandu Paandava poapa minjyale poayithu Kaavêri thaayina poayithu bodi eNduthuLLô nangara jaagathina Paandavang'k kodthiro kodthirthe. Akka Kaavêri thaayi ningak'ke ningara jaagathina kaathu beppi en'ndu eNNittu kaNi maadittu Paandavanga banthu jaagak'kêpaka, ninga botthu kutthir... kutthith'injiri, akka ninga ningara jaagathina ningakaaythe neenga botthu kutthiththu uLLira en'ndu naan Paandavang'k eNNuvi en'ndu ayichath. Baya Paandavanga banthu kêpaka Kodoava ikkaakale banthu botthu kutthirthi, idhu aingara jaaga aaypochi. Ninga idhe samayath bappakaala baariya en'ndu eNNichilo. Annane Paandavanga poana kuri ella botthu kandronja, Kaavêri thaayi aingak'ke kaNi maadri'nja Kodoavara jaagathina Kodoavak'ke kaathu rakshane maadi kodthaththu en'ndu kathe injathe. Nithin, kaNi poojera bagge chenna'NNithari? KaNi pooje eN'chingi nanga bhoomi thaayina pooje maadôvanthadhu. Ee theertha bandittu aana pithyaandu, ponjatheek'ke ee poojena maaduva. Manera poNNaaLa athava muttheyidhiya ee poojena maadôvanthadhu oru paddhathi. Poojek'ke onnaadu muppade poayittu poo kojjittu poona oru thattêl bechittu porame bechittippa. Enangan'chingi aa samayath manji budhiththu poorakodil manji budhithirôndu'ndu. Allinja pithyaandu ponjatheek'ke kôLi koova nêrakk'ke, soorya udhaya aapangincha minynya, poaytha poona oLuk'edthan bandittu manek'ke, naLLakki naDubaadel' mukkaalil' thaLeya thakki poLacha bechittu, allinja aa adhara kodiil' ee poona vechitt' alankaara maadittu, allinja akkira kodiil' ondo thenge, illengi boLLari, athava chauthe, êdhe'ondhaana edthandu, adhathil' nangara chôntha chekôsthrathina thrikôraNa aakaarathil' madakkiittu, allinja adhathil' ee thenge aadi boLLarinaade vasthrathil' alankaara maadittu, allinja ang' kodiyaale, muund kodiyaale, pinynya adikyena meedhiyaaythu bechittu, aanda allinja adhung'ke Kodoava maala aanantha kokkethaathi athava jômaale illengi patthaak'ke, pinynya karthadh' athava chôpp' athava pachche kuppi baLe bechittu alankaara maaduva. Allinja manel'uLLa ellaaru eddhu bandittu, kuLichittu bandittu dhêvaLa bôdiyandu, gurukaaranana thottiyandu, akki ittithth' ee poojena kaippa. Adhaana pinynya, muttheyidhiye poayittu Ganga pooje maadittu bappa. Manera pakkath'uLLa kanthaarek'ke poayittu, akki, pinynya kodiyele adike ittittu Ganga'na pooje maadittu, neerana pooje maadittu, allinja neer balchôndu bappa. Adhaaytthu gurukaaranang'k meedhi beppantha paddhathi injathe. Meedhi beppak'ke andh' akkakkale maadantha dhôse, pinynya kumbaLakari, pinynya thenge poLi, bella, inthaan'edtthandu gurukaaranang'k meedhi bechittu, adhallathe, aa meedhina edth'poayittu bêlil' kutthi kutthi bechantha botth'ra kôdil' bechirônja, gurukaaranana muund kuri kaakittu bappantha adhu oru paddhathi injathe. Allinja majjaNathra mêle, ee poojek'ke baLisiyantha poona, okkuvantha neer, thôdil'aadu poLel'aadu okkichiduva. Inyor vishaya illi eNNônduN'chingi, andathra dhina Kodoava saadharaNa aaythu tharkaarine thimbaalathe erchi meenu thimbala. Aachingi vaarandhu ooril' meen kari maadôvantha paddhathi injathe'ndu naan kêttiye. Enangan'chingi meenu Kaavêrammera prasaadha, poLenja kittuvanthadhu Kaavêrammera prasaadha en'ndu koodi sumaar jana nambith. SankramaNa aaythaana pinynya, eLiyavu periyaayinda thaanra samsaara kaara bendukade manek'ke poayittu periyayinda kaal pudichittu bappantha oru paddhathi injathe. Allinja paththu dhivasa kathane'kke sarvasaadhaaraNa aaythu bêl paNi aadu, boare oru paNi, nelathina kaLapanthadhu ondhu maadu'le. Ee samayathil' jana bendukadel' poayittu aasheervaadha padedhandu bappa. Alinja paththu dhivasa kayinja pinynya bappanthadhu Patthalôdhi. Patthalôdhi oru baari nalla oru punya dhina en'ndu nanga nambi'ndu, êdheng shubha kaarya uLLanthadhu, mane kettôdhaadu, inyane guddali pooje aadu, intha kelasa kaarbaarana nanga ee Patthalôdhi andhu maaduva. Adh aana pinynya, gurukaaranang'k meedhi beppanthadhu Tholeyaar thingathil' kaaranam koduppadhadu, gurukaaranang'k meedhi beppantha nammena sametha nanga nadathuva. Kaavêri Changraandi namme nangadellaara manek'ke pinynya manak'ke nallarike pinynya Kodoavaame tharad'ndu naanu bôdiyovi.
english: Nithin, why do you think the little botth was planted there? Do you know what is special about it? Pramod, do you know what planting a botth means? Go ahead and tell us. I heard a great story about this botth and wanted to share it. Once upon a time, in this Tholeyaar month, a sapling sprouted on its own in the cultivated field, right as the paddy was coming up. It is said that this bird would come, eat it, and damage it — and that is why, to keep the bird away, this botth is planted, as I have heard. This botth is like a sentinel in the field — it is a tree grown on the branch of another tree, specifically a ponge tree, growing on the branch of some tree in a good location. The kaibili baLLi creeper wraps around it... that creeper, much like it clings to things, has spread and stayed green without fading even now, in that same good spot. It became a botth by encircling it — that is its significance. The boare... I also heard that in a place full of boare trees, all the boare trees gathered together along with others. It is planted not only in the field but also in all the other important places — in the manner of the house, near the small elders' place, near the patthaaya, at ChaNakundra — everywhere. The reason for this is said to be that if the eyes of evil forces fall upon something, planting the botth keeps them away. Beyond that, there is another story — it is said that during the Pandavas' exile, they came to Kodagu, and the people of Kodagu were very happy to have them. Thinking they wanted to take our land and settle here in our kingdom, Kaaveri Thayi went ahead to this place and stood in their path in the middle of their route, and the Kodavas around there, hearing this, stood behind the Pandavas — Kaaveri Thayi stood at the front, and so the Pandavas, caught between them, were made to go away and were given the Kodavas' land. Thinking that Kaaveri Thayi was protecting this land for you and your people, she performed kaNi pooje, and when the Pandavas came to that place, you had planted the botth — having planted it, you stayed, protecting your land; and I will tell the Pandavas that you planted the botth saying this land is yours and you are here to stay, that is how it came to be. Fearing the Pandavas, the Kodavas came here and planted the botth first, and thus this became their land. One should not think that this occasion is merely an ordinary annual event. Just as it is told in the story that when the Pandavas left, Kaavêri, the mother, performed kaNi for all five of them and watched over and protected the Kodavas in the Kodava land, so it has been passed down as a tale. Nithin, have you heard about the kaNi pooje? The kaNi pooje is the worship we perform for our mother earth. It is the practice that, when the theertha arrives in a given year, the unmarried young women and the muttheyidhi women perform this pooje. It is a tradition that the women of the household — whether unmarried daughters or muttheyidhis — perform this pooje. For the pooje, one goes out beforehand, gathers flowers, and keeps them arranged on a plate, setting it aside. At that time, one understands that the mist rises and fills the paddy fields with dew. Then, on that day, just before the rooster crows and before sunrise, having gone out and come back with paddy stalks freshly plucked, placing them at the center of the naLLakki in the middle of the house, tilting the head to three-quarters, and placing the paddy stalks there, then at the other end placing a coconut, or boLLari, or chauthe, or some such one thing, folding one's own family's chôsthra into a triangular form, then decorating that coconut or boLLari with the cloth, and then at that spot placing the kodi, the muund kodi, and the pinynya adikye above them, and from the men's side placing a Kodava maala, aanantha kokke, jômaale, or patthu, and then placing the pinynya karthadh or chôpp or a green kuppi baLe and decorating. Then all those in the house rise up, come, bathe, bow before the dhêvaLa, touch the gurukaarana, offer akki, and perform this pooje. After that, the muttheyidhi goes and performs the Ganga pooje and returns. Going to the kanthaar beside the house, placing akki and the pinynya kodi adike, performing the Ganga pooje, performing the water worship, and then bringing water back. That is then placed over the gurukaarana according to the practice of meedhi beppe. For the meedhi beppe, the items prepared include dhôse made in the prescribed manner, pinynya kumbaLakari, pinynya thenge poLi, and bella — these are placed as meedhi over the gurukaarana; additionally, it is the practice that the meedhi is taken and offered at the botth's side where it is placed after being pounded in the bêlu, and that the gurukaarana's muund kuri is brought back marked. Then after the majjaNa, the paddy stalks that were offered for this pooje and the water that was gathered are poured out in the thôdi or the poLe. On a related note, in those days it was quite common among Kodavas to roast and eat fish without washing it first. I have heard that in some villages, there was no tradition of making fish curry at all. Some people believed that fish, once caught, was the prasaada of Kaavêramma, and that fish obtained after casting the net was also her prasaada. After Sankramana, there was a tradition where everyone in the household, from the youngest to the eldest, would go to the elders' side of the house, touch the feet of the elders, and return home. For about ten days after that, it was generally customary to engage in agricultural work such as weeding, clearing the fields of weeds, and similar tasks — all as one unit. During this time, people would go to the elders' homes, receive blessings, and return. After those ten days had passed, the next auspicious occasion was Patthalôdhi. We believe Patthalôdhi to be a truly auspicious and sacred day, and so any auspicious events such as house-warming ceremonies, starting construction with the guddali pooje, and similar important tasks are performed on this day. After that, the occasion that follows is Tholeyaar thinga, the month during which tribute is paid to the gurukaarana, and we observe the customary rites associated with paying respect to the gurukaarana. Finally, I wish to convey that the Kaavêri Changraandi festival is a time that brings goodness to all our homes, and that it is truly the Kodava way of life.
explanation: Kaveri Sankramana botth and kaNi pooje ritual paragraph. Botth tradition — scarecrow with Ponge tree. Kani pooje — married women worship Kaveri at dawn. Patthalodhi auspicious day. Native speaker audio.

kodava: Nithin, pinya botthu kuttho eN'chingi enth? Adara visheshathe enth'dha chenna'NNithari?
english: Nithin, why do you think the small botth was planted there? Do you know what is special about it?

kodava: Pramod, botth eNNo padathra artha, poadi
english: Pramod, do you know what the word botth means? Go ahead and tell us.

kodava: Ee botth'ra bagge naanu dhandu kathe eNNod'k kêttiye.
english: I heard a great story about this botth that I wanted to share.

kodava: Ondu enthaN'chingi ee Tholeyaar thingathil' natta bêlil boLe bandittu, nellu porame bappantha samaya.
english: Once, during the Tholeyaar month, a sapling sprouted on its own in the cultivated field, right at the time when the paddy was coming up.

kodava: Akka ee pakshi ella bandittu adhana thindittu kardu maaduva, adhana dhoora bekkôndu en'ndu ee botth'na kutthuvanthadhu naanu eNNodhu kêtti.
english: I heard that this bird would come and eat it and cause damage, and that to keep the bird away, this botth is planted.

kodava: Ee botth enthaN'chingi, marathra koalil' koalu, adhu sumaar jaagathil' ponge mara eNNo oru marathra koalina anga baLisiyava.
english: This botth is like a branch upon a branch of a tree — it is a growth that has grown on the branch of another tree, specifically a ponge tree, in a good location.

kodava: Aanda kôdik'ke kaibili baLLi... adhu kaibili baLLina ennange koottiyôNa adhara thoppu sumaar samayakathane'k'ke oNangathe pachche aaythe ippalo.
english: The kaibili baLLi creeper clings to it... just as that creeper wraps around things, its foliage has remained lush and green without wilting, even now in that same good season.

kodava: Adhana suththittu botthaaythu adhana beppa.
english: By encircling it, it became a botth — that is its significance.

kodava: Boare... achchengi sumaar boare ooril' boare boare marathina anga poNena ella koottiyava on sametha naanu eNNodhu kêttiye.
english: The other one... I also heard that in a place full of various [trees], all those on the branches of the different trees gathered there together.

kodava: Adhu bêlil allathe, baaki mukhyapatta sthaana, enchingi manera pattiil', pinya kanthaarera pakka, patthaayathra pakka, chaaNakundralli, ella beppa.
english: It is planted not only in the field but also in all the other important places — at the entrance of the house, near the elders' place, near the patthaaya, at ChaNakundra — everywhere.

kodava: Adhu enangan'chingi, ennengi dhushta shakthira kaNNu paduvantha injathengi, adhana dhoora bekkôndu en'ndu botthu kutthuva en'ndu kêttiye.
english: The reason for this, as I have heard, is that if the evil eye of malevolent forces falls upon a place, the botth is planted to drive it away.

kodava: Adhallathe inyor kathe entha uLLa enchingi ee Paandavanga vanavaasathra samayathil' Kodagu'k banthu aingak'ke baari Kodaguna baari khushi aayrth'lo.
english: Beyond that, there is another story — it is said that when the Pandavas came to Kodagu during their time of exile, the people of Kodagu were very pleased to receive them.

kodava: Akka nanga idhana nangara raajyak'ke koottiyôndu eNNeN'ndu avu Kaavêri thaayina poayith ee jaagak'kêkaa'ndu aingara madhyathile thakka bar'nand'ippaka, alli sutthu mutthu inja Kodoava adhana kêttandu Paandava poapa minjyale poayithu Kaavêri thaayina poayithu bodi eNduthuLLô nangara jaagathina Paandavang'k kodthiro kodthirthe.
english: Fearing that the Pandavas might settle here and claim our kingdom as their own, Kaaveri Thayi went ahead to this place and stood in their path in the middle of their route, and the Kodavas around there, hearing this, stood firm behind the Pandavas — with Kaaveri Thayi ahead and the Kodavas behind, the Pandavas, caught between them, were turned away, and thus our land was not given to the Pandavas.

kodava: Akka Kaavêri thaayi ningak'ke ningara jaagathina kaathu beppi en'ndu eNNittu kaNi maadittu Paandavanga banthu jaagak'kêpaka, ninga botthu kutthir... kutthith'injiri, akka ninga ningara jaagathina ningakaaythe neenga botthu kutthiththu uLLira en'ndu naan Paandavang'k eNNuvi en'ndu ayichath.
english: Thinking that Kaaveri Thayi was watching over and protecting this land for you, kaNi pooje was performed, and when the Pandavas came to that place, you had planted the botth — having planted it, you stood your ground — and it was declared: I will tell the Pandavas that you planted the botth to show that this is your land and that you are here to stay.

kodava: Baya Paandavanga banthu kêpaka Kodoava ikkaakale banthu botthu kutthirthi, idhu aingara jaaga aaypochi.
english: Before the Pandavas arrived, the Kodavas came here and planted the botth first, and thus this land became theirs.

kodava: Ninga idhe samayath bappakaala baariya en'ndu eNNichilo.
english: Do not think that this occasion is merely an ordinary yearly event.

kodava: Annane Paandavanga poana kuri ella botthu kandronja, Kaavêri thaayi aingak'ke kaNi maadri'nja Kodoavara jaagathina Kodoavak'ke kaathu rakshane maadi kodthaththu en'ndu kathe injathe.
english: Just as the story goes that when the Pandavas departed, Kaavêri, the mother, performed kaNi for all five of them, she has watched over and protected the Kodavas in the Kodava land and given them her protection.

kodava: Nithin, kaNi poojera bagge chenna'NNithari?
english: Nithin, have you heard well about the kaNi pooje?

kodava: KaNi pooje eN'chingi nanga bhoomi thaayina pooje maadôvanthadhu.
english: The kaNi pooje is the worship that we perform for our mother earth.

kodava: Ee theertha bandittu aana pithyaandu, ponjatheek'ke ee poojena maaduva.
english: In the year that this theertha arrives, the unmarried young women perform this pooje.

kodava: Manera poNNaaLa athava muttheyidhiya ee poojena maadôvanthadhu oru paddhathi.
english: It is a tradition that the unmarried daughters of the household or the muttheyidhi women perform this pooje.

kodava: Poojek'ke onnaadu muppade poayittu poo kojjittu poona oru thattêl bechittu porame bechittippa.
english: Before the pooje, one goes out earlier, gathers flowers, places the paddy stalks on a plate, and sets it aside outside.

kodava: Enangan'chingi aa samayath manji budhiththu poorakodil manji budhithirôndu'ndu.
english: In the same way, at that time, the mist rises and the paddy fields remain filled with mist.

kodava: Allinja pithyaandu ponjatheek'ke kôLi koova nêrakk'ke, soorya udhaya aapangincha minynya, poaytha poona oLuk'edthan bandittu manek'ke, naLLakki naDubaadel' mukkaalil' thaLeya thakki poLacha bechittu, allinja aa adhara kodiil' ee poona vechitt' alankaara maadittu, allinja akkira kodiil' ondo thenge, illengi boLLari, athava chauthe, êdhe'ondhaana edthandu, adhathil' nangara chôntha chekôsthrathina thrikôraNa aakaarathil' madakkiittu, allinja adhathil' ee thenge aadi boLLarinaade vasthrathil' alankaara maadittu, allinja ang' kodiyaale, muund kodiyaale, pinynya adikyena meedhiyaaythu bechittu, aanda allinja adhung'ke Kodoava maala aanantha kokkethaathi athava jômaale illengi patthaak'ke, pinynya karthadh' athava chôpp' athava pachche kuppi baLe bechittu alankaara maaduva.
english: Then in that year, for the unmarried young women, just before the rooster crows and before sunrise, having gone out and come back with freshly plucked paddy stalks, placing them at the center of the naLLakki in the middle of the house with the head tilted to three-quarters, and at that lower end placing the paddy stalks and decorating them, then at the upper end taking a coconut, or if not boLLari, or chauthe, or some such one thing, folding one's own family's chôsthra into a triangular shape over it, and then decorating that coconut or boLLari with the cloth, then placing the kodi, the muund kodi, and the pinynya adikye as meedhi above them, and from the men's side placing a Kodava maala, aanantha kokke, jômaale or patthu, and then placing the pinynya karthadh or chôpp or a fresh green kuppi baLe and decorating.

kodava: Allinja manel'uLLa ellaaru eddhu bandittu, kuLichittu bandittu dhêvaLa bôdiyandu, gurukaaranana thottiyandu, akki ittithth' ee poojena kaippa.
english: Then everyone in the house rises up, comes, bathes, bows before the dhêvaLa, touches the gurukaarana, offers akki, and performs this pooje.

kodava: Adhaana pinynya, muttheyidhiye poayittu Ganga pooje maadittu bappa.
english: After that, the muttheyidhi herself goes and performs the Ganga pooje and returns.

kodava: Manera pakkath'uLLa kanthaarek'ke poayittu, akki, pinynya kodiyele adike ittittu Ganga'na pooje maadittu, neerana pooje maadittu, allinja neer balchôndu bappa.
english: Going to the kanthaar beside the house, placing akki and adike on a small kodi leaf, performing the Ganga pooje, performing the water worship, and then bringing water back.

kodava: Adhaaytthu gurukaaranang'k meedhi beppantha paddhathi injathe.
english: After that, there is the practice of placing meedhi over the gurukaarana.

kodava: Meedhi beppak'ke andh' akkakkale maadantha dhôse, pinynya kumbaLakari, pinynya thenge poLi, bella, inthaan'edtthandu gurukaaranang'k meedhi bechittu, adhallathe, aa meedhina edth'poayittu bêlil' kutthi kutthi bechantha botth'ra kôdil' bechirônja, gurukaaranana muund kuri kaakittu bappantha adhu oru paddhathi injathe.
english: For the meedhi beppe, taking the items prepared in the prescribed manner — dhôse made with akki, pinynya kumbaLakari, pinynya thenge poLi, and bella — and placing them as meedhi over the gurukaarana; and apart from that, it is also the practice that the meedhi is taken and placed at the botth's side after being pounded in the bêlu, and the gurukaarana's muund kuri is brought back marked.

kodava: Allinja majjaNathra mêle, ee poojek'ke baLisiyantha poona, okkuvantha neer, thôdil'aadu poLel'aadu okkichiduva.
english: Then after the majjaNa, the paddy stalks that were offered for this pooje and the water that was collected are poured out in the thôdi or the poLe.

kodava: Inyor vishaya illi eNNônduN'chingi, andathra dhina Kodoava saadharaNa aaythu tharkaarine thimbaalathe erchi meenu thimbala.
english: On a related note, in those days it was quite common practice among Kodavas to roast and eat fish without washing it first.

kodava: Aachingi vaarandhu ooril' meen kari maadôvantha paddhathi injathe'ndu naan kêttiye.
english: I have heard that in some villages, there was no tradition at all of preparing fish as a curry.

kodava: Enangan'chingi meenu Kaavêrammera prasaadha, poLenja kittuvanthadhu Kaavêrammera prasaadha en'ndu koodi sumaar jana nambith.
english: In that way, quite a number of people believed that fish itself is the prasaada of Kaavêramma, and that whatever is obtained after casting the net is likewise her prasaada.

kodava: SankramaNa aaythaana pinynya, eLiyavu periyaayinda thaanra samsaara kaara bendukade manek'ke poayittu periyayinda kaal pudichittu bappantha oru paddhathi injathe.
english: After Sankramana, there was a tradition where everyone in the household, from the youngest to the eldest, would go to the elder relatives' home, touch the feet of the elders, and then return.

kodava: Allinja paththu dhivasa kathane'kke sarvasaadhaaraNa aaythu bêl paNi aadu, boare oru paNi, nelathina kaLapanthadhu ondhu maadu'le.
english: For about ten days after that, it was generally customary not to do agricultural work or other such tasks which involves tilling the land.
explanation: From kaveri_sankramana_transcription.txt, section section_04. maadu'le is the nonpast negative of maaduw'k — the -'le suffix marks negation. The sentence describes a customary rest from field work for ten days after the Kaveri Sankramana festival.

kodava: Ee samayathil' jana bendukadel' poayittu aasheervaadha padedhandu bappa.
english: During this time, people would go to the homes of their elders, receive blessings, and return.

kodava: Alinja paththu dhivasa kayinja pinynya bappanthadhu Patthalôdhi.
english: After those ten days had passed, the next occasion that came was Patthalôdhi.

kodava: Patthalôdhi oru baari nalla oru punya dhina en'ndu nanga nambi'ndu, êdheng shubha kaarya uLLanthadhu, mane kettôdhaadu, inyane guddali pooje aadu, intha kelasa kaarbaarana nanga ee Patthalôdhi andhu maaduva.
english: Believing Patthalôdhi to be a truly auspicious and sacred day, we perform all important ceremonies on this day — be it a house-warming, starting construction with the guddali pooje, or any other such significant undertaking.

kodava: Adh aana pinynya, gurukaaranang'k meedhi beppanthadhu Tholeyaar thingathil' kaaranam koduppadhadu, gurukaaranang'k meedhi beppantha nammena sametha nanga nadathuva.
english: After that comes Tholeyaar thinga, the month in which tribute is paid to the gurukaarana, and during this time we observe all the customary rites associated with showing reverence to the gurukaarana.

kodava: Kaavêri Changraandi namme nangadellaara manek'ke pinynya manak'ke nallarike pinynya Kodoavaame tharad'ndu naanu bôdiyovi.
english: I wish to convey that Kaavêri Changraandi is a festival that brings goodness and wellbeing to all our homes, and that this is truly the way of the Kodava people.
